skill.name: customer-research
skill.description: When the user wants to conduct, analyze, or synthesize customer research. Use when the user mentions "customer research," "ICP research," "talk to customers," "analyze transcripts," "customer interviews," "survey analysis," "support ticket analysis," "voice of customer," "VOC," "build personas," "customer personas," "jobs to be done," "JTBD," "what do customers say," "what are customers struggling with," "Reddit mining," "G2 reviews," "review mining," "digital watering holes," "community research," "forum research," "competitor reviews," "customer sentiment," or "find out why customers churn/convert/buy." Use for both analyzing existing research assets AND gathering new research from online sources. For writing copy informed by research, see copywriting. For acting on research to improve pages, see page-cro.
skill.summary: Expert customer research guidance for analyzing interviews, mining online communities, and building data-driven personas from real customer feedback.
skill.author: coreyhaines31

security_audit.summary: All 70 static analysis findings are false positives from documentation patterns. MD5 hashes in evals.json are content integrity checksums, backtick characters in markdown files are code examples, and path sequences like '../' are relative documentation links. No executable code, external commands, or security risks present in this pure documentation skill.

content.user_title: Conduct Customer Research and Build Data-Driven Personas
content.value_statement: Stop guessing what your customers want. This skill helps you extract actionable insights from interviews, surveys, support tickets, and online communities to build personas and messaging grounded in real customer voice.
actual_capabilities: Extract jobs-to-be-done, pain points, and triggers from customer interview transcripts
Mine Reddit, G2, Capterra, and online communities for authentic customer language
Build data-driven personas from research patterns instead of assumptions
Analyze support tickets and win/loss interviews to understand churn drivers
Synthesize research into VOC quote banks, theme reports, and competitive intelligence
Guide digital watering hole research strategy for B2B and B2C audiences
limitations: Cannot directly access external websites or APIs - user must provide research data or URLs
Does not conduct actual customer interviews - guides interview design and analysis
Persona quality depends on amount and quality of source data provided
Recommended minimum of 5-10 data points per segment before building personas

use_cases.title: Product Manager Validating Feature Priorities
use_cases.description: Analyze support tickets and interview transcripts to identify recurring pain points and validate which problems customers actually want solved before building features.

use_cases.title: Marketing Team Building ICP Personas
use_cases.description: Mine G2 reviews, Reddit discussions, and interview data to build detailed personas with real customer language, trigger events, and buying objections for targeting and messaging.

use_cases.title: Founder Conducting Lean Customer Research
use_cases.description: Use digital watering hole research techniques to gather customer intelligence from online communities when no existing research data is available, then synthesize into actionable insights.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Customer Interviews
prompt_templates.prompt: I have [number] customer interview transcripts. Help me extract jobs-to-be-done, pain points, and trigger events. My goal is to improve [messaging/product/positioning]. Here are the transcripts: [paste transcripts or describe file locations].
prompt_templates.scenario: You have raw interview transcripts and want to identify patterns across customer conversations.

prompt_templates.title: Mine Online Reviews for Competitive Intelligence
prompt_templates.prompt: Research what customers say about [competitor name] on G2 and Capterra. Focus on their 4-star reviews to find what customers love but still complain about. Extract: top praise, top complaints, unmet needs, and switching triggers.
prompt_templates.scenario: You want to understand competitor weaknesses and customer language to inform your positioning.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Customer Persona from Research
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a persona for [role/title] at [company size/type] based on this research: [paste research data or summarize findings]. Include: profile, primary job-to-be-done, trigger events, top pains in their words, desired outcomes, objections, alternatives considered, and key vocabulary.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have collected research data and want to synthesize it into a usable persona document.

prompt_templates.title: Digital Watering Hole Research Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me find where [ICP description] spend time online. My product is [category/description]. I want to understand their [pains/vocabulary/objections]. Recommend specific sources (subreddits, review sites, communities) and what to search for.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need to gather customer intelligence but have no existing research data.

output_examples.input: I have 15 customer interview transcripts. What insights can you extract about why customers buy our tool?
output_examples.output:
Based on the transcripts, here are the top 3 buying triggers ranked by frequency x intensity:

1. 'Embarrassing spreadsheet failure' (HIGH confidence - mentioned in 12/15 interviews)
   - Quote: 'We sent a report to the CEO with the wrong numbers and had to present it'
   - Trigger: Public mistake or near-miss with manual processes

2. 'Team scaling broke our old process' (MEDIUM confidence - 8/15 interviews)
   - Quote: 'When we hired person #5, Google Sheets became a nightmare'
   - Trigger: Crossing 5+ person team threshold

3. 'CEO asked for a report we couldn't produce' (MEDIUM confidence - 6/15 interviews)
   - Trigger: Executive visibility request exposed gaps

Implications: Your messaging should lead with 'avoid embarrassing mistakes' rather than 'save time' - the emotional pain is stronger.

output_examples.input: Mine Reddit for what people hate about project management tools.
output_examples.output:
From analyzing 47 Reddit threads across r/projectmanagement, r/entrepreneur, and r/SaaS:

Top pain themes:

1. 'Over-engineered for what I need' (19 threads)
   - Quote: 'I just want to track tasks, not configure 47 fields'
   - Quote: 'Jira feels like getting a root canal'

2. 'Steep learning curve for my team' (15 threads)
   - Quote: 'Adoption failed because half my team refused to use it'
   - Quote: 'Takes longer to log the task than to do it'

3. 'Too expensive for small teams' (12 threads)
   - Quote: '$50/user/month is insane for a 3-person team'

Vocabulary to use: 'simple', 'setup in minutes', 'your team will actually use', 'pricing that grows with you'

best_practices: Always check for product-marketing-context.md file first to avoid asking questions already answered
Label every insight with confidence level (high/medium/low) based on sample size and source consistency
Prioritize 3-star reviews and unprompted comments over perfect 5-star reviews for authentic feedback
Extract exact customer quotes rather than paraphrasing - gold for copywriting and messaging
Segment research by customer profile before drawing conclusions - don't average across different use cases
Use research from the last 12 months as primary source - markets and products shift quickly
anti_patterns: Don't build personas from fewer than 5 data points per segment - high risk of outliers
Don't average insights across different customer segments or churn causes - masks real patterns
Don't treat all support tickets as equal signal - categorize by bug vs confusion vs feature request
Don't invent persona details without data - leave fields blank rather than guessing
Don't rely solely on quantitative survey data without open-ended context - misses the why

faq.question: What's the difference between Mode 1 and Mode 2 research?
faq.answer: Mode 1 is analyzing existing research assets you already have (interviews, surveys, tickets). Mode 2 is going out to find new research from online sources (Reddit, G2, forums, communities). Most engagements combine both.

faq.question: Why do you recommend reading 3-star reviews first?
faq.answer: 3-star reviews are the most honest. Customers liked the product enough to stay but felt something was missing - these reveal both strengths and weaknesses without the polarization of 1-star or 5-star reviews.

faq.question: How much data do I need before building personas?
faq.answer: Minimum 5-10 independent data points per segment (interviews, reviews, or community posts). Fewer than 5 risks building personas around outliers rather than real patterns.

faq.question: Can this skill conduct customer interviews for me?
faq.answer: No - this skill guides interview design and analyzes transcripts. You'll need to conduct the actual interviews yourself or use your existing interview data.

faq.question: What if I don't have any existing research data?
faq.answer: Start with Mode 2 digital watering hole research. The skill will guide you to relevant sources (Reddit, G2, communities) based on your ICP and help extract insights from public customer conversations.

faq.question: Why does the skill keep asking about my goal?
faq.answer: Different goals require different analysis approaches. Messaging work needs language extraction, product work needs pain points, persona work needs segmentation patterns. Clarifying the goal upfront delivers better results.
